note: Legacy book — research.json backfilled only for the concepts this pass touched (control-protocol, concurrency, verify-integration). Earlier concepts (01-09) predate the research artifact and are intentionally not backfilled.

sources.title: LinkLog Control Protocol reference implementation (Go, agent-side client)
Python SDK High-Level Design — §4 Concurrency: How the SDK Handles Simultaneous Requests
P08b: Agent Integration Rules (llog rules sync + llog verify-integration)
DR-018: Agent Rules Are Composed From One Source and Gated Against Drift

structure.rationale: LCP is the fleet-coordination protocol between the Go sidecar (agent) and the commercial control plane — the counterpart to LGP (SDK->agent) covered in ch.9. A Python-SDK integrator never implements LCP, but must understand it for two reasons: it is a persistent outbound connection from inside their network to a vendor server, so its safety shape matters; and it is what decides whether a fleet of their services survives a correlated storm rather than opening 10,000 PRs. The chapter is therefore framed around the three load-bearing rules (envelope, conclusions-not-commands, safe-to-lose), the storm-containment mechanism the whole protocol exists for, the policy floor, and what all of it concretely means for the reader (default-off, local audit log, locally-pinned key, local caps as the always-on floor).
structure.audience: Working Python developers integrating LinkLog who already wired up capture (ch.1-9) and now want to understand and safely enable the control channel.

claims.text: LinkLog has two protocols: LGP (SDK->Agent, forensic execution graphs, <1ms hot-path budget, one artifact per failure) and LCP (Agent<->Control Plane, coordination, a few low-volume messages per minute, never on a hot path). They version independently.

LCP exists for one reason request/response cannot serve: per-agent failure containment does not compose across a fleet. Ten thousand agents each correctly deduping the same fingerprint locally still produce ten thousand pull requests for one bug; fleet-wide caps need a coordination point.

The Envelope Principle (the load-bearing rule): the agent's capability envelope is declared locally and enforced locally; the control plane may only request within it. The control plane can never grant a capability the local config did not already allow — it may only make the agent do less, never more.

Server->agent messages carry verdicts and limits (conclusions and constraints), never commands or data requests. Structurally enforced: no LCP message may name a filesystem path, a query, or an executable; messages reference fingerprints, artifact IDs, and lease IDs only. 'If a message can name a file, it is the wrong message.'

Every platform->agent message must be safe to lose. The channel may drop or reset; the agent must degrade to a known-good state (ask again, or fall back to local caps), never a wrong one. Delivery is at-most-once: no acknowledgment protocol, no redelivery, no receiver dedup obligation.

Transport is agent-initiated outbound only — the agent never listens, the customer opens no inbound ports. v1 normative binding is WebSocket over wss:// with subprotocol 'lcp.v1', mTLS mutually authenticated, JSON encoding, one JSON message per text frame, max message size 256 KiB.

role is a closed set: production | ci. A production agent always has executes_plugins=false and serves_context_requests=false; ci may have executes_plugins=true but never serves_context_requests. A production agent advertising executes_plugins=true is malformed and the platform must reject the session.

Fleet storm containment has two mechanisms. Correctness: ACTION_REQUEST/ACTION_GRANT/ACTION_DENY/ACTION_COMPLETE — the agent asks before any expensive/outward action (LLM call, PR, ticket); a grant is a time-bounded lease issued to exactly one agent. Broadcast budgets alone cannot do this (telling 10,000 agents '1 remaining' yields 10,000 actions).

A grant is a lease: expiry is the only backstop (server sets expires_at generously above the action's worst case), no renewal, leases survive disconnects (time-scoped, not session-scoped, held locally with lease_id + expires_at). Only expiry or ACTION_COMPLETE ends a lease.

ACTION_DENY reasons are a closed normative set: already_claimed (do not act), budget_exhausted (do not act), stand_down_active (do not act), unknown_action (act per local caps — fail open; the channel must never gain a veto over actions it does not understand).

STAND_DOWN is the storm optimization layered on the ACTION_REQUEST correctness mechanism: once a fingerprint is claimed the platform proactively silences the fleet instead of fielding 10,000 requests. The agent clamps 'until' to now + max_stand_down_s (default 3600, locally configurable; the platform cannot see, set, or exceed it). Sustained suppression requires continuous re-issuing, each occurrence landing in the customer-owned audit log.

The Floor Rule: effective rules = local rules union platform layer. The customer's local ruleset is the floor and is never touched, extended, shadowed, or referenced by a push. Each POLICY_TIGHTEN carries the complete platform layer (not a delta), wholesale-replacing the prior platform layer. More redaction is safe; less is a trust inversion — only POLICY_TIGHTEN exists, POLICY_REPLACE is forbidden.

POLICY_TIGHTEN is protected by an Ed25519 signature over the decoded blob bytes exactly as decoded (no JSON canonicalization anywhere in LCP); the receiver verifies bytes first, parses second. The authoritative version lives inside the signed blob. The verifying public key is local configuration placed by the customer's deploy — LCP can never carry a key, since a channel that rotates its own trust root grants itself capability.

Agents must enforce five floor rules on POLICY_TIGHTEN or reject: (1) no pushed rule id may collide with a local rule id, (2) the update must not touch capture config/sampling/allowlist mode, (3) signature must verify against the locally pinned key, (4) inner version must exceed the stored version (monotonic; replay rejected), (5) every applied or rejected update is written to a customer-owned local audit log. POLICY_ACK reports applied or rejected with a closed reason set.

Forbidden types are permanently reserved and must be rejected: EXECUTE_PLUGIN (RCE / command-and-control shape), FETCH_CONTEXT (data-exfiltration primitive), CAPTURE_MORE (remotely loosening capture inverts the trust boundary), POLICY_REPLACE (would permit removing redaction), and any message naming a path/query. Receivers must reject unknown message types and log the rejection — unknown types are not forward-compatible extension points.

Against a fully hostile control plane: code execution and host-data exfiltration are rejected (forbidden types, not implemented agent-side); weakening sanitization is rejected (floor rule + signature); rotating the trust root is impossible (keys never travel the channel); broadening capture is out of scope. The one residual risk is suppression — possible but bounded by the max_stand_down_s clamp, visible in the audit log, and it can only withhold actions, never leak data.

The LCP client ships default-off in the open-source agent — it is disabled unless the operator turns it on. When the channel is down the agent must still capture, parse, sanitize, fingerprint, diagnose and dispatch; it fails open to local per-agent caps (the SDK storm caps from ch.7). Air-gapped is a first-class deployment, not a degraded one.

The v1 frozen message set (17 types): HELLO, HELLO_ACK, HELLO_REJECT, HEARTBEAT, GOODBYE, FAILURE_REPORT, ACTION_REQUEST, ACTION_GRANT, ACTION_DENY, ACTION_COMPLETE, STAND_DOWN, ACTION_BUDGET, POLICY_TIGHTEN, POLICY_ACK, CORRELATION, ENVELOPE_VIOLATION. Additive fields and additive 'action' enum values do not bump the version; new message types do.

snippets.note: The envelope block is the agent telling the platform what it may not ask; the platform cannot expand it. Verified against spec §4.1.
Agent clamps until to now + max_stand_down_s (default 3600). Verified against spec §5.3 and containment.go handleStandDown.

snippets.code:
max := c.Now().UnixMilli() + c.MaxStandDownSeconds*1000
clamped := p.Until > max
until := min(p.Until, max)
c.standDowns[p.Fingerprint] = until
snippets.note: lcp/containment.go — the local-maximum clamp is unconditional; the platform cannot see or exceed it.

pitfalls: Reading LCP as a command channel. It carries verdicts and limits only; a message that could name a file, query, or executable is by construction the wrong message.
Assuming the channel must be up for LinkLog to work. It is default-off and safe-to-lose; the SDK's local caps are the always-on floor and the channel is pure enhancement.
Confusing LCP with LGP. LGP is SDK->agent forensic capture on the hot path (ch.9); LCP is agent<->control-plane coordination and never touches the application.

claims.text: Every in-flight request gets its own Session -> its own Buffer -> its own file descriptor and its own causality graph. There is no process-wide buffer requests write into together, so 'hundreds at once' is just hundreds of independent objects. The only shared state is read-mostly config plus two deliberately-locked coordination points (the storm limiter and one-time directory setup).

Thread-per-request (WSGI/sync): @llog.intent calls _begin() (tracker.py) which builds a fresh Session and stores it with _session.set(s), where _session is a ContextVar so each thread reads its own value. The current parent eid is a second ContextVar (_parent), so nested with llog.scope(...) blocks build the correct parent->child graph per thread with no cross-talk. Each Buffer holds one O_TMPFILE fd and writes go straight to the kernel via os.write (no application-memory buffering, invariant #2), so concurrent threads never touch the same fd.

Async/FastAPI is the case the design is built around: FastAPI interleaves many requests on a single event-loop thread. The tracker uses contextvars rather than a thread-local or module global because a threading.local is shared across coroutines that interleave on the same thread and a module global is shared across everything — either would splice two concurrently-interleaved requests' execution graphs into one artifact, a correctness bug AND a privacy leak (invariant #5), one that only appears under concurrency. contextvars gives each async task its own copy of _session and _parent.

The one shared write path is a per-buffer lock. Within a single buffer there can be two writers — the request thread and the (M4) watchdog thread that commits deadlocked requests that never return (invariant #9). Two threads appending into one MessagePack stream would corrupt the artifact, so every Buffer carries its own threading.Lock (taken by write/discard/commit) plus a closed-state flag making post-commit writes no-ops. Because the lock is per buffer it is never a contention point between requests — a request contends only with its own watchdog; uncontended acquisition is ~20-25ns and must be benchmarked with the watchdog live (DR-001, unverified until M4).

Simultaneous concurrency is not the scary scenario — correlated failure is (invariant #8). When a shared dependency dies, all N requests fail together and all N buffers try to commit, filling /dev/shm and handing the sidecar N identical artifacts for one bug; the architecture would invert under exactly the load it exists to survive. The per-signature storm limiter (ratelimit.py) contains this: key is the cheap local signature exc_type|innermost_frame|intent (not the sidecar's authoritative fingerprint, DR-011), per-signature token bucket default 10 commits/min (max_commits_per_min), one shared globally-locked object, suppressed counts stamped as V_SUPPRESSED_COUNT (counted not lost), fails open (invariant #13), bucket map LRU-evicted at 4,096 so a storm of distinct signatures can't OOM the app.

Resource ceilings bound hundreds->thousands: one fd per in-flight buffer (fd count is a real budget at high concurrency, HLD §9), and the root fd for linkat plus directory setup are opened once per process (_get_root_fd, _ensure_dirs), never per request, to not double it on the hot path. tmpfs pages count against the container's cgroup limit so each buffer is capped (MAX_BUFFER_BYTES, MAX_EVENTS) — an unbounded buffer could OOM-kill the very app it observes (invariant #7). Zero panic throughout: a full /dev/shm, an fd closed underneath, a limiter bug — every path degrades to 'stop recording' or 'allow the commit', never a raise into application code.

The concurrency mechanism runs as described only on Linux: O_TMPFILE, /dev/shm, and the linkat commit exist only on Linux; macOS/Windows silently use the named-file fallback and the suite refuses to collect there. The concurrent-request behavior is verified on real Linux via the linux-test harness, not on a developer laptop — a concurrency stress test that 'passes' on macOS proved nothing about the mechanism the product is built on.

snippets.code:
# tracker.py — per-request isolation via contextvars, NOT thread-locals
_session: ContextVar[Session | None] = ContextVar("llog_session", default=None)
_parent:  ContextVar[str | None]     = ContextVar("llog_parent",  default=None)

def _begin(intent_name):
    s = Session(intent_name)        # fresh Session -> fresh Buffer -> fresh fd
    _session.set(s)                 # each thread AND each async task: own copy
    return s
snippets.note: Conceptual, faithful to python.md §4.1-4.2: contextvars give both threads and interleaved coroutines their own _session/_parent.
FastAPI/ASGI is the concurrency case the design targets. Verified against python.md §4.2.

pitfalls: Assuming a thread-local (or a module global) would work. Under async both splice two interleaved requests into one artifact — a correctness bug and a privacy leak that only appears under concurrency.
Thinking mere simultaneity is the stress case. It isn't — correlated failure is; N buffers committing at once is what the per-signature limiter exists to contain.
Trusting a concurrency stress test that passes on macOS. The mechanism (O_TMPFILE/linkat/dev-shm) doesn't exist there; only the Linux harness proves it.

claims.text: A coding agent can integrate a LinkLog SDK wrongly in ways that look like they work: it reaches for a global sys.excepthook that a web framework's exception handling silently pre-empts (invariant #9), it verifies on a Mac laptop and gets a false green because the core capture path doesn't exist there, and it picks ANONYMOUS mode and never captures the OOM-kills that are the whole point in Kubernetes (invariant #10). Every one is invisible to an agent reading the SDK and obvious only on execution.

llog rules sync reads the bundled composed rules file (offline, no network) and merges it into the project-root AGENTS.md inside <!-- BEGIN llog-python vX -->...<!-- END llog-python --> markers. It is idempotent and marker-scoped, so running it twice is byte-identical and a polyglot repo gets one clean block per SDK; --also CLAUDE.md is optional. It carries the SDK version and warns if the installed SDK is newer than an existing block.

The rules block redefines 'done': integration is complete not when the code compiles but when llog verify-integration forces a real failure and observes a .llog land on disk on Linux. The rules steer the agent to framework middleware as the primary path (not excepthook), the explicit ANONYMOUS-vs-STAGING buffer-mode choice tied to its OOM consequence, allowlist capture mode as the default for regulated environments, the write-only boundary, and 'zero-panic means internal errors are swallowed, so a caught exception is not proof of capture — assert the artifact, not the return value.'

llog verify-integration is a self-contained mechanism smoke test: it instruments a tiny function, forces a raise, and asserts a .llog appears in /dev/shm/llog/events/ (and, if a sidecar is watching, that it is picked up — but 'no sidecar' is still a normal PASS since it asserts only the SDK-side commit). It is not a phone-home: it produces a local artifact and inspects it locally via the reader path, never reading state back through the SDK (write-only preserved, invariant #6).

On macOS verify-integration refuses with a plain-language explanation of why a green there would be false (no O_TMPFILE, no /dev/shm — only the named-file fallback), exiting non-zero rather than reporting success. The refusal is the correct outcome and is load-bearing: a mutation check stubs the platform guard to 'allow' and confirms it would false-green on the fallback. Exit codes are three distinct values: 0 = verified/synced, 1 = ran but no artifact (capture broken), 3 = refused (wrong platform), so a caller can tell 'broken' from 'not applicable here'.

DR-018: agent rules are composed from one canonical source (usage-rules/core.md, language-neutral integrator voice, plus a per-language overlay such as overlays/python.md) into a committed bundle shipped inside the SDK package (sdk/python/llog/_rules/rules.md), and CI holds the bundle to its source and to reality — it regenerates the bundle via tools/compose_rules.py and fails on any diff, and the Linux harness executes the integration snippet embedded in the composed rules plus verify-integration, asserting a checkpoint lands. For a product whose thesis is 'prose drifts from truth', shipping unmanaged integration prose would be dogfooding the failure; the executable done-gate is what makes the rules more than assertion.

The audience distinction is the load-bearing design call: CLAUDE.md is for the project's own contributors' agent, while usage-rules/core.md is for the user's integrator agent — same facts, different cut, keeping only what bites someone adding LinkLog. The Java overlay (2026-07-19) proved the compose machinery generalizes and surfaced a new requirement: an overlay must be honest about feature parity (Java lacks allowlist/storm-limiting/watchdog that the shared core states as facts), pinned by a test so silence can't invite the agent to invent missing APIs.

snippets.code:
$ llog rules sync
  wrote <!-- BEGIN llog-python v1 --> block into ./AGENTS.md
  (idempotent: re-running is byte-identical; --also CLAUDE.md optional)

$ llog verify-integration        # on Linux
  forcing a failure through your instrumentation...
  PASS  artifact observed: /dev/shm/llog/events/3f9c.llog   (exit 0)

$ llog verify-integration        # on macOS
  REFUSED  no O_TMPFILE / no /dev/shm here — a green would be false.  (exit 3)
snippets.note: Faithful to P08b §6 exit criterion + §9 exit-code contract (0 verified / 1 capture broken / 3 refused).

pitfalls: Treating 'the code compiles / the exception was caught' as done. Zero-panic swallows internal errors, so a caught exception is not proof of capture — assert the artifact on disk.
Trusting a green verify on macOS. There is no green on macOS by design: it refuses (exit 3) because the capture mechanism doesn't exist there.
Hand-editing the rules block or the bundled rules.md. The bundle is composed from usage-rules/core.md + the overlay and CI fails on drift; edit the source, regenerate.

volatile: max_commits_per_min default (currently 10) and the LRU bucket cap (currently 4,096)
per-buffer lock uncontended cost (~20-25ns, unverified until M4 per DR-001)
verify-integration exit codes (0 / 1 / 3) and the AGENTS.md marker version (currently v1)
the closed 'action' enum values (ai_diagnose | create_issue | create_pr | create_ticket | notify)
